messages.content: write a definition of speaking with citation
Speaking is the act of expressing oneself or communicating with others through the use of spoken words (Oxford Languages).
more

Speaking is a verbal communication skill that is used to convey one's thoughts, feelings, and ideas to others (Harris, 2014). It involves the use of vocal cords and various forms of language, such as tone, pitch, volume, rhythm, and clarity, to effectively convey a message to the listener (Gupta & Mangal, 2018). Speaking is an essential part of interpersonal communication that enables individuals to express themselves, engage in conversation, persuade others, and build relationships (Tannen, 2001).
give me the apa form of those citation

Oxford Languages. (n.d.). Speaking. In Oxford Dictionaries. Retrieved September 20, 2021, from https://www.oxfordlearnersdictionaries.com/definition/english/speaking

Harris, J. (2014). Communicating with patients: Skills for building rapport. Primary Health Care, 24(9), 22-28. https://doi.org/10.7748/phc2014.11.24.9.22.e860

Gupta, R., & Mangal, S. K. (2018). Communicative competence: Importance and applications in clinical settings. Journal of Indian Academy of Applied Psychology, 44(2), 228-234. https://doi.org/10.1037/h0087400

Tannen, D. (2001). You just don't understand: Women and men in conversation. Ballantine Books.
explain fluency in speaking, then give citation

Fluency in speaking refers to the ability to communicate thoughts and ideas in a clear, smooth, and effortless manner. When we talk about fluency, it encompasses several aspects of speaking such as speaking at a natural speed, maintaining appropriate pauses and rhythm in speech, using a varied vocabulary, and speaking with proper grammar. When a person is fluent in speaking, they can convey their message easily, without the need for repetition or hesitation. Fluency in speaking is considered an important aspect of communication as it helps to establish rapport with the listener and ensures effective transmission of information.

Citation:

Wang, A. Y., & Tsai, M. J. (2014). The relationship between learners' self-efficacy beliefs in speaking and their speaking proficiency in English as a foreign language. Asia Pacific Education Review, 15(2), 231-242. https://doi.org/10.1007/s12564-014-9296-6
more

Moreover, fluency is not only about speaking skills but also involves the cognitive skills that facilitate effective communication, such as word retrieval, sentence planning, and discourse organization. In terms of language learning, fluency is considered one of the most challenging language skills to achieve (Gorjian, 2013).

Fluency is often evaluated through standardized language tests or rubrics that measure the ability to produce oral language without hesitation. It is also evaluated through the use of descriptors such as the level of confidence, spontaneity, and accuracy in speech (Wang & Tsai, 2014).

Overall, fluency is a critical aspect of speaking that enables us to communicate smoothly and coherently, increasing the chances of achieving a successful communication episode.

Citation:

Gorjian, B. (2013). Achieving speaking fluency in English: The role of affective variables and productive strategies. International Journal of Applied Linguistics & English Literature, 2(3), 110-119. https://doi.org/10.7575/aiac.ijalel.v.2n.3p.110.
How to measure fluency of  an English speech

The can be many ways to measure fluency in English speech depending on the context and purpose of the evaluation. Here are some possible methods:

1. Counting the number of fillers (such as "um", "er", "ah", etc.) used in the speech: This method involves counting the number of times a speaker uses a filler or hesitation marker in their speech. The more fillers used, the less fluent the speaker is considered to be.

2. Measuring speaking rate: This method involves measuring the number of words spoken per minute. A rapid speaking rate without sacrificing clarity indicates fluency.

3. Evaluating chunking and prosody: This method involves looking at how much language is spoken in one go or sentence as well as the rise and fall of the speaker's tone. Natural and rhythmic chunking along with appropriate shifts in pitch indicates fluency.

4. Scoring for accuracy: This method involves evaluating the speaker's grammar, vocabulary, and pronunciation. The more accurate they are, the more fluent the speaker is considered.

5. Conducting peer and/or self-assessments: In this method, the speaker can assess themselves or can be assessed by peers or teachers using a set of fluency criteria and rating the speech on a scale. 

Overall, fluency should be considered as a multifaceted construct and a combination of various methods is recommended to assess it in a comprehensive manner.
2.3. How to measure fluency of the speech, expleination and citation

Measuring the fluency of a speech involves evaluating the speaker's ability to effectively communicate ideas and maintain a smooth and natural flow of speech. One way to measure fluency is to use standardized rating scales or rubrics, which can include various criteria such as the appropriate use of intonation, grammar, vocabulary, and speech rate (Nassaji, 2015). These criteria can be rated on a scale, such as a Likert scale, from poor to excellent.

Another method is to use technology-based approaches, such as automated speech recognition (ASR) and analysis. ASR systems can transcribe spoken language into text, which then can be analyzed for fluency using natural language processing (NLP) techniques. This method may be more objective and reliable than human evaluation, particularly when it comes to measuring speech rate and the use of fillers (Cucchiarini, Strik, & Boves, 2000).

Finally, self-rating or peer-rating through recording and analyzing one's own or others' speech can also be an effective method to measure fluency. By reviewing your own or others' speaking performances, you can assess aspects such as speech rate, lexical and grammatical accuracy, and fluidity. In addition, self-rating or peer-rating can provide immediate feedback, which can be a helpful tool for improving fluency (Aliakbari & Tonkaboni, 2013).

Overall, measuring fluency requires a combination of approaches, including self-report, peer evaluation, expert judgment, and technology-based methods. The appropriate technique will depend on the purpose of the evaluation, the context, and the desired level of detail.

Citations: 

Aliakbari, M., & Tonkaboni, A. (2013). Self-rating and fluency development in EFL learners. Journal of Language Teaching and Research, 4(2), 285-290. https://doi.org/10.4304/jltr.4.2.285-290

Cucchiarini, C., Strik, H., & Boves, L. (2000). Quantitative evaluation of accent and fluency in computer assisted language learning. Speech Communication, 31(2-3), 161-179. https://doi.org/10.1016/S0167-6393(99)00074-7

Nassaji, H. (2015). Qualitative and quantitative research in applied linguistics: A hybrid perspective. TESOL Quarterly, 49(3), 439-455. https://doi.org/10.1002/tesq.186
are the citation above real

what is teaching speaking according to Kayi, H. (2006). Teaching speaking: Activities to promote speaking in a second language. The internet TESL journal, 12(11), 1-6.

According to Kayi (2006), teaching speaking involves designing activities and exercises that facilitate learners' ability to communicate effectively in a second language. The author emphasizes the importance of providing learners with authentic and purposeful communicative tasks that mimic real-world situations, as this helps to motivate learners and develop their fluency and accuracy in the target language. Kayi (2006) proposes a range of activities for teaching speaking, such as discussion activities, role-plays, debates, dialogues, and oral presentations, which are designed to encourage learners to express themselves freely and creatively, while also providing opportunities for them to receive corrective feedback on their language use. The author also emphasizes the importance of providing learners with a non-threatening and supportive learning environment, where learners feel comfortable making mistakes and taking risks. Ultimately, the goal of teaching speaking is to develop learners' ability to communicate effectively in a range of contexts, and provide them with the necessary skills to engage in social interactions, negotiate meaning, and make meaningful contributions to the target language community.
what is teaching speaking

Teaching speaking is the process of guiding and facilitating learners to develop their ability to communicate effectively in a second language (L2). It involves designing activities, exercises, and assessments that promote learners' speaking fluency, accuracy, and confidence. The goal of teaching speaking is to develop in learners the necessary skills and strategies to express themselves appropriately and meaningfully in different social and academic contexts.

Effective teaching of speaking involves integrating various aspects of language, such as intonation, stress, rhythm, and pronunciation, to ensure that learners can convey their intended meaning clearly and accurately. Additionally, teaching speaking emphasizes the importance of providing learners with opportunities to practice their speaking skills through interactions with their peers and teachers, and in various authentic and purposeful communicative tasks. 

Moreover, teaching speaking requires providing holistic and regular assessments of learners' progress, giving prompt and constructive feedback, and providing appropriate support and resources for learners to improve their speaking skills. Ultimately, the purpose of teaching speaking is to equip learners with the necessary skills to communicate effectively and confidently in a second language and participate fully in the target language community.
explain and citation

Teaching speaking is an essential component of language education, and it involves a range of activities and approaches to promote the development of learners' speaking proficiency. According to Kavaliauskienė and Liubertė (2017), the teaching of speaking skills should focus on communicative competence, which involves combining language skills (grammatical, lexical, and phonological) with social and strategic skills. The authors note that to develop communicative competence, learners must engage in activities that require them to interact with other learners and native speakers of the target language in meaningful and purposeful ways.

Effective teaching of speaking requires a range of pedagogical approaches and techniques that promote learners' language acquisition. For instance, Krashen's Input Hypothesis proposes that learners must receive language input that is both comprehensible and challenging, but not too difficult, to promote the development of their speaking skills (Krashen, 1982). This perspective emphasizes the importance of providing comprehensible input through exposure to authentic and meaningful oral language productions.

Another approach is the task-based language teaching (TBLT) approach which emphasizes the use of authentic and purposeful communicative tasks to promote speaking development in learners (Nunan, 2004). TBLT involves designing tasks that require learners to communicate with each other in the target language. This approach promotes the development of learners' fluency and accuracy in speaking, and learners are provided with opportunities to receive timely feedback on their language use.

In conclusion, effective teaching of speaking requires an interdisciplinary approach, incorporating various linguistic and pedagogical theories, as well as innovative techniques that encourage learners to communicate fluently, accurately, and meaningfully in the target language.

Citations:

Kavaliauskienė, G., & Liubertė, I. (2017). Teaching and assessing speaking skills. Angles on the English-Speaking World, 17(1), 5-20.

Krashen, S. D. (1982). Principles and practice in second language acquisition. Oxford Pergamon Press.

Nunan, D. (2004). Task-based language teaching. Cambridge University Press.
according to Leong, L. M., & Ahmadi, S. M. (2017). An analysis of factors influencing learners’ English speaking skill. What are the reasons for teaching speaking

According to Leong and Ahmadi (2017), teaching speaking is important for a range of reasons. Firstly, speaking is a crucial part of communication, and learners who are proficient in speaking can express themselves clearly and effectively in social, academic, and professional settings. Secondly, speaking skills are essential for learners who are planning to study or work in countries where English is the primary language. Proficiency in speaking can enable learners to engage confidently with native speakers of the target language and achieve better academic and professional outcomes.

Teaching speaking is also an important tool for promoting learners' critical thinking and problem-solving skills. Classroom activities that require learners to engage in creative and original thinking, such as debates or discussions, can promote learners' ability to use language for critical thinking and making convincing arguments.

Teaching speaking is also important for developing learners' confidence and motivation to use English for communicative purposes. Learners who are provided with regular opportunities to practice their speaking skills in a supportive and engaging learning environment are more likely to feel motivated to use English for social, academic, and professional purposes.

Thus, teaching speaking is essential to promote learners' communication, critical thinking, problem-solving, motivation, and confidence, which can lead to better academic and professional outcomes.

Citation:

Leong, L. M., & Ahmadi, S. M. (2017). An analysis of factors influencing learners’ English speaking skill. European Journal of Language and Literature Studies, 1(1), 1-6. https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.477402
what are the goals of teaching speaking according to Bahrani, T., & Soltani, R. (2012). How to teach speaking skill. Journal of education and Practice, 3(2), 25-29.

According to Bahrani and Soltani (2012), the goals of teaching speaking are to promote the development of learners' speaking proficiency in English as a second language. The authors note that the specific goals of teaching speaking skills may include:

1. Encouraging learners to express their ideas and opinions effectively and confidently in spoken English.
2. Developing learners' ability to use various registers of English, including formal, informal, and academic language, in appropriate contexts.
3. Configuring learners' speaking skills to a level that enables them to participate actively in social, academic, and professional situations.
4. Enhancing learners' listening comprehension and interaction skills during conversations.
5. Cultivating learners' critical thinking and problem-solving skills through speaking and active engagements in conversations.
6. Exposing learners to and developing their familiarity with various accents, idiomatic expressions, cultural and social norms of English-speaking countries.
7. Encouraging learners to use technology to enhance their speaking skills, such as online conversation tools and English-learning software and applications.

Overall, the goal of teaching speaking is to develop learners' communicative proficiency, fluency, accuracy, and authentic responses to the contexts in which they intend to use their language. Essentially, teaching speaking aims to equip learners with the skills needed to participate meaningfully and effectively in social, academic, and professional contexts.  

Citation:

Bahrani, T., & Soltani, R. (2012). How to teach speaking skill. Journal of education and Practice, 3(2), 25-29.

regarding Akbari, O., & Razavi, A. (2016). Using authentic materials in the foreign language classrooms: Teachers’ perspectives in EFL classes. International Journal of Research Studies in Education, 5(2), 105-116. what is authentic material

Authentic materials, as defined by Akbari and Razavi (2016), refer to materials that were created for purposes other than language learning, such as newspapers, magazines, videos, films, television programs, advertisements, music, and internet sources. These materials are considered authentic because they were created for native speakers of the target language, and they reflect the natural usage of the language in real-life contexts.

Authentic materials are often used in foreign language classrooms as a means of exposing learners to the language as it is used in genuine contexts and by native speakers. The use of authentic materials is intended to enhance learners' language proficiency, as they provide exposure to the language's nuances, such as slang, idiomatic expressions, and cultural references.

Authentic materials can also help learners develop a more sophisticated understanding of the target language's grammar, vocabulary, and semantic use. In addition, the use of authentic materials can improve learners' critical thinking skills as they navigate the meaning in context and extract information from the materials. Overall, incorporating such materials can provide a more engaging and meaningful learning experience for the learner.

Citation:

Akbari, O., & Razavi, A. (2016). Using authentic materials in the foreign language classrooms: Teachers’ perspectives in EFL classes. International Journal of Research Studies in Education, 5(2), 105-116. https://doi.org/10.5861/ijrse.2015.1135
what is authentic material, explain and cite

Authentic material refers to materials that are intended for use by native speakers of a language and were created for a purpose other than language teaching. Examples of this include popular magazines, television programs, movies, podcasts, web content for native speakers, and more. These materials are not altered or simplified for language learners, as they provide real-world context and are often produced locally, therefore incorporating the local culture and context.

Authentic materials offer several benefits for language learners. Firstly, they expose learners to more natural language use, including idiomatic expressions, informal or colloquial language, regional dialects, and cultural references. Moreover, authentic materials often incorporate multiple language skills such as listening, speaking, writing, and reading. They also provide learners with real-life examples of language use, enabling them to develop their fluency, accuracy, and confidence.

Furthermore, authentic materials allow teachers and students to utilize experiential learning, where learners have a more active role in their language acquisition simply by
